annotations.label: Clock arithmetic example

anchor.snippet: If the hour hand points to 7 now, then 8 hours later it will point to 3

annotations.note: Informal motivating arithmetic example not stated as a Mathlib lemma.

annotations.label: 12 hours wait identity

anchor.snippet: After a wait of exactly 12 hours, the hour hand will be right where it started

annotations.note: Informal example; no dedicated lemma in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Congruence modulo m

anchor.snippet: two integers a and b are said to be congruent modulo m , if their difference a − b is an integer multiple of m

annotations.note: `Int.ModEq n a b` is defined exactly as a % n = b % n, with the equivalent dvd characterization in `Int.modEq_iff_dvd`.

annotations.label: Congruence is a congruence relation

anchor.snippet: Congruence modulo m is a congruence relation , meaning that it is an equivalence relation compatible with addition , subtraction , and multiplication

annotations.note: Mathlib provides the equivalence-relation instance and the compatibility lemmas `Int.ModEq.add`, `.sub`, `.mul` (and analogues for `Nat.ModEq`).

annotations.label: Modulo operation (b mod m)

anchor.snippet: which refers to the remainder of b when divided by m , known as the modulo operation

annotations.note: Mathlib uses the core `Int.emod`/`Nat.mod` for the modulo operation.

annotations.label: Same remainder characterization

anchor.snippet: a ≡ b (mod m ) asserts that a and b have the same remainder when divided by m

annotations.note: By definition `Int.ModEq n a b ↔ a % n = b % n`.

annotations.label: Negative modulus equivalence

anchor.snippet: This means that every non-zero integer m may be taken as a modulus

annotations.note: `Int.ModEq` permits any integer modulus but there is no dedicated lemma asserting that the relation is unchanged under negation of the modulus.

annotations.label: 38 ≡ 14 (mod 12)

anchor.snippet: because the difference is 38 − 14 = 24 = 2 × 12 , a multiple of 12

annotations.note: Specific numerical example, not stated as a Mathlib lemma.

annotations.label: Congruence with negative values

anchor.snippet: The definition of congruence also applies to negative values

annotations.note: Illustrative example only.

annotations.label: Equivalence relation properties

anchor.snippet: The congruence relation satisfies all the conditions of an equivalence relation

annotations.note: Equivalence is captured by the `Equivalence` instance built from `Int.ModEq.refl`, `.symm`, `.trans` (line 87).

annotations.label: Compatibility properties of congruence

anchor.snippet: compatibility with translation

annotations.note: Compatibility with translation/scaling/multiplication is covered by `Int.ModEq.add`, `.add_left`, `.mul_left`, `.mul`, `.neg`, `.sub`, `.pow`.

annotations.label: Euler totient exponent rule

anchor.snippet: where φ is Euler's totient function , then a c ≡ a d (mod m ) —provided that a is coprime with m

annotations.note: `Nat.ModEq.pow_totient` gives `x^φ n ≡ 1 [MOD n]` when `x` is coprime with `n`, yielding the exponent-mod-φ(m) rule.

annotations.label: Congruence modulo product implies modulo factors

anchor.snippet: If a ≡ b (mod mn ) , then a ≡ b (mod m ) and a ≡ b (mod n )

annotations.note: `Int.ModEq.of_mul_left` and `Int.ModEq.of_mul_right` give exactly this.

annotations.label: Cancellation rules

anchor.snippet: For cancellation of common terms, we have the following rules

annotations.note: Cancellation results are in `Int.ModEq.mul_left_cancel'`, `.mul_right_cancel'`, and the gcd-aware `cancel_left_div_gcd` family.

annotations.label: Division within modular arithmetic

anchor.snippet: The last rule can be used to move modular arithmetic into division

annotations.note: The gcd-divided cancellation lemmas underpin moving to division, but no single lemma states the article's `(a/c) ≡ (b/c)` reformulation directly.

annotations.label: Modular multiplicative inverse

anchor.snippet: The modular multiplicative inverse is defined by the following rules

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `Inv (ZMod n)` (line 714) so that `a * a⁻¹ = 1` when gcd is 1, e.g. `ZMod.mul_inv_eq_gcd` / `ZMod.coe_mul_inv_eq_one`.

annotations.label: Computing multiplicative inverse via Bézout

anchor.snippet: may be efficiently computed by solving Bézout's equation

annotations.note: Bézout coefficients via `Int.gcdA`/`Nat.xgcd` exist, but Mathlib does not package an explicit `inverse via Bézout` algorithm/lemma for ZMod.

annotations.label: Inverses exist modulo a prime

anchor.snippet: thus a multiplicative inverse exists for all a that is not congruent to zero modulo p

annotations.note: Encoded by the `Field (ZMod p)` instance under `[Fact p.Prime]`, so every nonzero element is invertible.

annotations.label: Fermat's little theorem

anchor.snippet: Fermat's little theorem

annotations.note: `ZMod.pow_card` (line 583) and `ZMod.pow_card_sub_one_eq_one` (line 606) give Fermat's little theorem.

annotations.label: Euler's theorem

anchor.snippet: Euler's theorem

annotations.note: `Nat.ModEq.pow_totient` is exactly Euler's theorem `a^φ(n) ≡ 1 [MOD n]`.

annotations.label: Inverse via Fermat's little theorem

anchor.snippet: A simple consequence of Fermat's little theorem is that if p is prime

annotations.note: The underlying identity `a^{p-1}=1` is `ZMod.pow_card_sub_one_eq_one`, but Mathlib does not packaged this as a named formula for `a⁻¹ = a^{p-2}`.

annotations.label: Exponent congruence consequence

anchor.snippet: Another simple consequence is that if a ≡ b (mod φ ( m ))

annotations.note: Follows from `Nat.ModEq.pow_totient` but no dedicated `x^a ≡ x^b` lemma indexed by `a ≡ b [MOD φ m]` is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Wilson's theorem

anchor.snippet: Wilson's theorem

annotations.note: `ZMod.wilsons_lemma` gives `(p-1)! = -1 (ZMod p)` and `Nat.prime_iff_fac_equiv_neg_one` is the iff form.

annotations.label: Chinese remainder theorem

anchor.snippet: Chinese remainder theorem

annotations.note: `ZMod.chineseRemainder`/`Nat.chineseRemainder` formalize CRT; further generalizations exist as `Ideal.quotientInfRingEquivPiQuotient`.

annotations.label: Lagrange's theorem (polynomial mod p)

anchor.snippet: Lagrange's theorem

annotations.note: `Polynomial.card_roots` bounds roots of a nonzero polynomial over an integral domain by its degree, which specializes to the Z/p case.

annotations.label: Primitive root modulo m

anchor.snippet: Primitive root modulo m

annotations.note: Mathlib's general `IsPrimitiveRoot` plus `IsCyclic (ZMod n)ˣ` encode the notion of primitive root mod m.

annotations.label: Existence of primitive roots

anchor.snippet: A primitive root modulo m exists if and only if m is equal to 2, 4

annotations.note: `ZMod.isCyclic_units_iff` characterizes exactly when `(ZMod n)ˣ` is cyclic, i.e. primitive roots exist.

annotations.label: Quadratic residue

anchor.snippet: Quadratic residue

annotations.note: Quadratic residues mod p are formalized via `IsSquare (a : ZMod p)`, used e.g. in `ZMod.euler_criterion`.

annotations.label: Euler's criterion

anchor.snippet: Euler's criterion asserts that, if p is an odd prime

annotations.note: `ZMod.euler_criterion` states `IsSquare a ↔ a^(p/2) = 1` for `a ≠ 0` in `ZMod p`.

annotations.label: Congruence class / residue class

anchor.snippet: The equivalence class modulo m of an integer a is the set of all integers of the form a + k m

annotations.note: Residue classes are elements of `ZMod n` (equivalently `ℤ ⧸ Ideal.span {(n:ℤ)}`).

annotations.label: Unique representative in range

anchor.snippet: Each residue class modulo m contains exactly one integer in the range

annotations.note: Combined with `Int.emod_nonneg` and `Int.emod_emod_of_dvd`, `Int.emod_lt_of_pos` picks the unique representative in `[0, m)`.

annotations.label: Residue of a modulo m

anchor.snippet: it is called the residue of a modulo m

annotations.note: The residue is `a % m` (`Int.emod`/`Nat.mod`) and corresponds to `ZMod.val` in `ZMod n`.

annotations.label: Partition into residue classes

anchor.snippet: every integer belongs to one and only one residue class modulo m

annotations.note: Follows from the `Equivalence` instance on `Int.ModEq`; the quotient is `ZMod n`.

annotations.label: Least and complete residue systems

anchor.snippet: The set of integers {0, 1, 2, ..., m − 1} is called the least residue system modulo m

annotations.note: No dedicated `complete residue system` definition; Mathlib uses `ZMod n` directly.

annotations.label: Complete residue systems modulo 4

anchor.snippet: the least residue system modulo 4 is {0, 1, 2, 3}

annotations.note: Specific illustrative example, not a Mathlib lemma.

annotations.label: Non-complete residue systems mod 4

anchor.snippet: Some sets that are not complete residue systems modulo 4 are

annotations.note: Illustrative example only.

annotations.label: Reduced residue system

anchor.snippet: is called a reduced residue system modulo m

annotations.note: Mathlib has `(ZMod n)ˣ` and `ZMod.unitsEquivCoprime { x // Coprime x.val n }` rather than a 'reduced residue system' name.

annotations.label: Covering system

anchor.snippet: Covering systems represent yet another type of residue system that may contain residues with varying moduli

annotations.note: Covering systems are not defined in Mathlib (grep finds no occurrences).

annotations.label: Ring of integers modulo m

anchor.snippet: The set of all congruence classes modulo m is a ring called the ring of integers modulo m

annotations.note: `ZMod n` is defined in `Mathlib/Data/ZMod/Defs.lean` line 149.

annotations.label: Edge cases m = 0, 1

anchor.snippet: When m = 1

annotations.note: `ZMod 0 = ℤ` is realized by `IsDomain (ZMod 0)`/`CharZero (ZMod 0)`; `ZMod 1` is a `Subsingleton`.

annotations.label: Commutative ring structure

anchor.snippet: The properties given before imply that, with these operations

annotations.note: `ZMod n` carries a `CommRing` instance (inherited via `Fin n` / `ℤ`).

annotations.label: Quotient ring description

anchor.snippet: is used because this ring is the quotient ring

annotations.note: `Int.quotientSpanNatEquivZMod` provides the ring iso `ℤ ⧸ (n) ≃+* ZMod n`.

annotations.label: Cyclic group classification

anchor.snippet: All finite cyclic groups are isomorphic with

annotations.note: `zmodCyclicMulEquiv` shows any cyclic group is `MulEquiv` to `Multiplicative (ZMod n)`.

annotations.label: Z/m is a field iff m prime

anchor.snippet: The ring of integers modulo m is a field

annotations.note: `Field (ZMod p)` is the instance under `[Fact p.Prime]`; conversely a `ZMod n` field forces `n` prime.

annotations.label: Finite field of prime power order

anchor.snippet: there exists a unique (up to isomorphism) finite field

annotations.note: `GaloisField p n` plus `GaloisField.algEquivGaloisField` give existence and uniqueness of the finite field of order p^n.

annotations.label: Multiplicative group of integers modulo m

anchor.snippet: denotes the multiplicative group of the integers modulo m that are invertible

annotations.note: `(ZMod n)ˣ` is the multiplicative group; `ZMod.unitsEquivCoprime` identifies it with elements coprime to n.

annotations.label: ISBN/IBAN checksums

anchor.snippet: International Standard Book Number (ISBN) uses modulo 11

annotations.note: Application example, no ISBN/IBAN checksum formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Casting out nines

anchor.snippet: The method of casting out nines offers a quick check of decimal arithmetic computations performed by hand

annotations.note: Application example; no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Day of the week algorithms

anchor.snippet: Arithmetic modulo 7 is used in algorithms that determine the day of the week for a given date

annotations.note: Application example; no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Linear congruences in polynomial time

anchor.snippet: A linear system of congruences can be solved in polynomial time

annotations.note: Complexity statement, not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nonlinear modular system is NP-complete

anchor.snippet: Solving a system of non-linear modular arithmetic equations is NP-complete

annotations.note: NP-completeness result, not formalized in Mathlib.
